coredata.dc:title: Development of silica molecularly imprinted polymer on carbon dots as a fluorescence probe for selective and sensitive determination of cetirizine in saliva and urine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Yixuan
Su, Aoxuan
Feng, Jingying
Dong, Weichong
Li, Junmei
Wang, Huan
Ni, Xiaoyu
Jiang, Ye
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescence probe based on carbon dots (CDs) coated with silica molecularly imprinted polymer (MIPs) was synthesized for selective and sensitive determination of cetirizine (CTZ). Green source carbon dots were firstly derived from orange peels through a microwave method, and had the merits of eco-friendly and low toxicity. Then a thin silica film was formed on the surface of CDs by reverse microemulsion technique, and molecularly imprinted polymer coated on silica–carbon dots. In this scene, CTZ, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) were employed as a template, a functional monomer and cross linker, respectively. The obtained CDs-MIPs can selectively bind CTZ through the specific interaction between recognition sites and template, and obey photoinduced electron transfer fluorescence quenching mechanism. Fluorescence dropped linearly in the range of 0.5–500 ng mL−1, under the optimal conditions, with a detection limit of 0.41 ng mL−1. Furthermore, the proposed method was successfully intended for the determination of trace CTZ in human saliva and urine samples without the interference of other molecules and ions. And recoveries ranged from 95.8% to 99.8% with relative standard deviation less than 3.0%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Cetirizine
Reverse microemulsion method
Saliva and urine analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs from orange peel
Preparation and characterization of the CDs, CDs-MIPs and CDs-NIPs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank the foundation supported by National College Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (project No. USIP2020066 ) for funding this research. Furthermore, we would like to thank the Department of Electron Microscopy Center, Hebei Medical University for our TEM work and we would be grateful to Chenming Zhou for his help of make TEM samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Effect of time intervals with UV irradiation on FL intensity of CDs, (B) Effect of ionic strengths on the FL intensity of CDs, (C) Effect of pH on FL intensity of CDs.
TEM image of (A): CDs, (B): CDs-MIPs, and (C): CDs-NIPs.
(A): UV–vis absorption spectrum of CTZ, CDs and CDs-MIPs; (B): UV–vis absorption spectrum of extraction mixtures after each wash.

(A): FL spectra of the CDs at different excitation wavelengths; (B): Normalized FL spectra of CDs and CDs-MIPs at 360 nm as excitation wavelength.; (C): FT-IR spectra of CTZ, CDs, CDs-NIPs, and washed CDs-MIPs.
Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of CDs-MIPs in the absence (A) and presence (B) of CTZ.
(A): Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity; (B): Effect of the amount of CDs-MIPs on the fluorescence intensity; (C) Effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity.

Fluorescence spectra of (A) CDs-NIPs and (B) CDs-MIPs at the optimum conditions in the presence of different concentrations of CTZ; (C) Linear relationship between quenching efficiency (F0-F) of CDs-MIPs and CTZ concentration.
Comparison of fluorescence intensity of CDs-MIPs and CDs-NIPs after the addition of different species in the presence of 250 ng mL−1 CTZ.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of CD-MIPs.
Comparison of the developed sensor with the reported methods for the detection of CTZ in real samples.
Results of determination of CTZ in saliva and urine samples. (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Development of silica molecularly imprinted polymer on carbon dots as a fluorescence probe for selective and sensitive determination of cetirizine in saliva and urine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Hebei Medical University, Shijiazhuang, Hebei Province 050017, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Hebei Medical University, Shijiazhuang, Hebei Province 050017, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, The Second Hospital of Hebei Medical University, Shijiazhuang, Hebei Province 050000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Second Hospital of Hebei Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacy, The Second Hospital of Hebei Medical University, Shijiazhuang, Hebei Province 050000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescence probe based on carbon dots (CDs) coated with silica molecularly imprinted polymer (MIPs) was synthesized for selective and sensitive determination of cetirizine (CTZ). Green source carbon dots were firstly derived from orange peels through a microwave method, and had the merits of eco-friendly and low toxicity. Then a thin silica film was formed on the surface of CDs by reverse microemulsion technique, and molecularly imprinted polymer coated on silica–carbon dots. In this scene, CTZ, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) were employed as a template, a functional monomer and cross linker, respectively. The obtained CDs-MIPs can selectively bind CTZ through the specific interaction between recognition sites and template, and obey photoinduced electron transfer fluorescence quenching mechanism. Fluorescence dropped linearly in the range of 0.5–500 ng mL−1, under the optimal conditions, with a detection limit of 0.41 ng mL−1. Furthermore, the proposed method was successfully intended for the determination of trace CTZ in human saliva and urine samples without the interference of other molecules and ions. And recoveries ranged from 95.8% to 99.8% with relative standard deviation less than 3.0%.

ce:section.ce:para: Cetirizine (CTZ) is a carboxylated metabolite of hydroxyzine belonging to the second-generation antihistamines with H1histamine receptors, which is one of the most effectively and frequently used medications for the treatment of allergic symptoms such as allergic rhinitis, pollen hypersensitivity, sneezing and itching [1–3]. CTZ can be quickly absorbed upon oral administration in syrup or tablet forms and can be extensively distributed in the urine, saliva and other biofluids in the unmodified form [4]. However, it is well known that CTZ has a variety of side effects and severe effects including sedation, drowsiness or decreased cognitive processing even cardiac failure and tachycardia during the medication [5]. Hence, it is of great importance to determine the trace CTZ Due to the enormous interest of CTZ for therapeutic use.

Recently, various analytical methods have been developed for determination of CTZ in biological fluids including electrochemical sensor [6], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [7], potentiometry [8], high performance liquid chromatography-ultraviolet (HPLC-UV) [9], and HPLC in combination with tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS/MS) [10]. Nevertheless, most of these methods are lengthy, high cost, require complicated procedure and often need tiresome sample preparations. On the other hand, fluorescence detection of analyte is a very elegant method in analytical chemistry field [11]. Furthermore, the application of fluorescence sensors in pesticide residue monitoring, clinical assays or bioimaging is a major area of interest within the field [12–13]. Fluorescence sensors are endowed with various advantages like inherent specificity, fast response, high sensitivity and simplicity of preparation, which can satisfy the requirements for some tasks particularly for the determination of different type molecules, such as drugs in biological fluids [14–15]. Among the fluorescence sensors, carbon dots (CDs) are emerging material especially suitable for trace or ultra-trace detection. Till date, there is no relevant report on CTZ determination by employing CDs as fluorescence probe.

Since its accidental discovery, CDs has promptly attracted increasing attention in sensing analysis. It appeared as a new class of fluorescence nanomaterial regarding to its facile preparation, tunable emissions, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and high photostability [16–20]. And it is a kind of favorable nanomaterial to replace traditional materials such as semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes [21]. Furthermore, using natural products and wastes as carbon source for the synthesis of CDs gains more attention due to several merits especially green without addition of surface passive agents [22]. Therefore, CDs is considered as excellent response signal candidate for designing sensors. Whereas synthesis methods commonly applied (e.g., chemical oxidation method and hydrothermal method) using natural resources (e.g., citric acid, cedrus and glycine) are susceptible to the influencing factors like temperature, oxygen [23–24]. Additionally, it has problems such as weak anti-interference ability, poor selectivity, and low sensitivity. Hence, it is of great significant to achieve the selective detection of target analytes in complex systems and improve the anti-interference ability of the prepared CDs. And CDs possesses large quantities of functional groups on the surface such as hydroxyl and carboxyl, which makes for formation of modified CDs more easily.

Molecular imprinted polymers (MIPs) are materials for creating the molecular recognition sites that specifically bind well with the shapes, sizes, and function groups of the template molecules [25–28]. And it’s also suitable to modify the CDs by using molecular imprinting technique. Typically, the synthesis of MIPs can be obtained by different ways [29–30] such as precipitation polymerization, bulk polymerization, suspension polymerization and reverse micro emulsion method of which reverse micro emulsion technology offers a simple way to produce a 3D polymer network with high porosity, smaller particle size, excellent physical rigidity, higher binding capacity and faster mass transfer [31]. More importantly, the coated silicate MIPs on the surface have no impact on fluorescence performance of CDs. Inspired by these properties, we expect to construct a new MIPs-capped CDs fluorescence probe that can specifically and sensitively recognize and bind CTZ.

In the current work, a fluorescence probe based on MIPs on the surface of CDs (CDs-MIPs) was fabricated for determination of CTZ. Orange peel-based green source CDs were firstly organized by microwave method. Meanwhile, the MIPs polymers were prepared using reverse micro emulsion method. In the following, to prepare reinforced CDs with MIPs, a mixture of CDs, template molecules (CTZ), functional monomer (3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane, APTES), and crosslinker (tetraethoxysilane, TEOS) was used in the synthesis process with the attendance of ammonia solution as the catalyst. The proposed florescence probe, exhibited a high selectivity and specificity towards the CTZ and it was successfully applied for CTZ trace detection in real saliva and urine samples.

Cetirizine standard (≥99% purity grade) was from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd (China). Orange was purchased from local markets in Shijiazhuang. Triton X-100, tetraethoxysilane (TEOS, 98% purity grade), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, 99% purity grade), ammonium hydroxide solution (25–28%) of analytical-grade were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biotechnology Co., Ltd (China). Methanol and acetonitrile of HPLC grade (≥99.9% purity grade) solvents were from Fisher (Louhboriugh, UK). Other reagents, including cyclohexane (≥99.5% purity), acetone (≥99.5% purity), phosphoric acid (≥85.0 %, w/v), glacial acetic acid (≥99.6% purity), boric acid (99.5% purity) and sodium hydroxide (96.0% purity) of analytical-grade were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Ultrapure water was prepared using the Milli-Q50 water purification system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA).

All fluorescence spectra were recorded by a Shimadzu RF–6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). A Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR-8400S, Shimadzu, Japan) was employed to explore the surface functional groups using KBr pellets in the range of 250–4000 cm−1. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained with a T1901 double beam UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument., China) over the range of 200–650 nm.

The morphology of CDs, CDs-MIPs and CDs-NIPs was characterized by a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, HT7800, Hitachi, Japan) operating at 100 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was measured on a Rigaku Corporation X-ray diffractometer (Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs from orange peel
ce:section.ce:para: The biomass CDs were synthesized using orange peels as the carbon source by a microwave approach. Typically, the orange peels (1 g) were mixed with 20 mL of deionized water under ultrasound and then the mixture was added into a microwave digestion tank. Microwave irradiation was proceeded through a programmed heating process: start from room temperature to 180 °C, holding at 180 °C for 30 min. Afterwards, the cooled dark brown suspension solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particle and then filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane. Finally, the product was collected and stored at 4 °C.

The reverse micro emulsion was used to prepare imprinting polymer at the surface of CDs. Generally, 1.8 mL of Triton X-100 was added to 7.5 mL of cyclohexane under magnetic stirring for 30 min. Then 100 µL of ammonium hydroxide (25–28% v/v) and 400 µL CDs and 50 µL TEOS were successively added and the mixture was stirred for 2 h to prepare silica-coated CDs. Afterwards, 5.0 mg of CTZ and 20 µL of APTES were added to the above solution and the reaction system was kept under stirring for 12 h at room temperature. After reaction, 20 mL of acetone was used for breaking the microemulsion. Next, the resulting product was collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min and the obtained solid was washed thoroughly by a mixture solvent of acetonitrile/ethanol (v/v,2:8) to remove the original CTZ templates in the imprinted nanoparticles, until no CTZ could be detected from the washing solvent with UV–vis spectrometer. Finally, the CDs-MIPs were dried by vacuum. As a control, non-imprinted polymer microspheres (CDs-NIP) were prepared under the same conditions without addition of the CTZ.

The optical sensing properties of the CDs-MIPs and CDs-NIPs were evaluated under the following conditions: the fluorescence measurements were carried out with the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Typically, 1.0 mg of the CDs-MIPs or CDs-NIPs was dispersed into 2.0 mL of Britton-Robinson buffer (pH = 7.0, 0.001 M) under ultrasound. Next, 200 µL of CTZ standard solution at different concentrations was added to the above mixture. The fluorescence intensity was measured in the absence of CTZ recorded as F0 while F was measured in the presence of CTZ after 4 min incubation.

Human blank saliva and urine samples were collected from volunteers in agreement with the “Ethics Committee of Andalusian’s Biomedical Research” [32], and the Declaration of Helsinki. The Ethic Review Committee of Hebei Medical University approved all of the procedures (approval no. 2016032). In order to remove the proteins of saliva, 2 mL of saliva sample and 2 mL of 10% (w/v) trichloroacetic acid were mixed in a centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was then filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane. Next, the obtained sample was ten-fold of their original concentrations diluted with the BR buffer solution (pH = 7.0). Eventually, 200 µL of the saliva sample was added to 2 mL of the suspension containing 500 µg mL−1 of the CDs-MIPs (pH = 7.0) and CTZ was determined using the fluorescence measurements. The urine pooled samples were centrifuged for 20 min at 5000 rpm, filtered through 0.22 μm membrane and no further treatment was needed. 200 µL of the real urine sample treated just like saliva samples. In addition, all the samples were analyzed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the CDs, CDs-MIPs and CDs-NIPs
ce:section.ce:para: In the procedure of CDs-MIPs preparation, silica layer was firstly introduced to modify CDs by hydrolyzing TEOS at the cyclohexane/water interface, catalyzed by ammonium hydroxide [33]. Afterwards, CTZ molecule (template) was organized into the polymers by the hydrogen bond interactions and van der Waals forces, employing APTES as functional monomers. After removal of the template, the CDs-MIPs can rebind the CTZ molecules owing to the formation of three-dimensional cavities.

The morphology of CDs, CDs-MIPs and CDs-NIPs were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 A, it could be observed that the CDs exhibited as spherical and well dispersed particles. The particle size of the resulting CDs-MIPs and CDs-NIPs particles showed similar appearances and larger than that of original CDs, additionally, both of them displayed spherical nanostructure with approximately 60 nm diameter and relatively narrow size distributions. The XRD patterns of CDs was shown in Fig. 3 , a broad peak at around 2θ = 20.5 that according with the (002) diffraction planes of carbon materials implied an amorphous structure of the CDs [33].

Typical UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs and CDs-MIPs was shown in Fig. 4 A. Furthermore, the CTZ molecules were removed by ultrasonic assisted through repeated washing with 30 mL of acetonitrile/ethanol (v/v,2:8) solvent, each time for 15 min, as shown in Fig. 4B, after 4 times washing procedure, CTZ is not detectable by the UV–vis spectrophotometer.

The fluorescence spectrum of the CDs under different excitation wavelengths from 300 to 400 nm was demonstrated in Fig. 5 A. As a result, the synthesized CDs exhibited strong blue photoluminescence in the fluorescence emission spectra and the emission were highly dependent on the excitation wavelength. Furthermore, the fluorescence emission peak shifted to longer wavelength and the fluorescence intensity increased when the excitation wavelength increased from 300 to 360 nm and reached peak value at the wavelength of 360 nm. While excitation wavelength of 360 nm was observed as the maximum of emission wavelength, this wavelength was selected as the excitation wavelength for all subsequent experiments. Moreover, Fig. 5B illustrated the normalized fluorescence spectrum of the CDs and washed CDs-MIPs at a 360 nm excitation wavelength. It’s obvious that the CDs-MIPs emission position was a little red-shifted compared to the original CDs, which might result from the size increase of CDs during the polymerization.

FT-IR spectroscopy was applied to characterize the surface functional groups on CTZ, CDs, CDs-MIPs and CDs-NIPs. The FT-IR spectrums of CTZ, CDs, CDs-NIPs and washed CDs-MIPs were investigated and compared. As shown in Fig. 5C, for CDs, the broad peak at 3400 cm−1 was related to the –OH stretching vibrations. The absorption bands at 1590 cm−1 and 1350 cm−1 were assigned to bending vibration and stretching vibrations of –OH and –NO2 group. Besides, the bands at 1700 cm−1 were associated with the stretching vibration of C = O. For CDs-MIPs and CDs-NIPs, the strong and broad absorption peaks at about 1050 cm−1 corresponded to Si-O-Si asymmetric stretching and the peak at about 810 cm−1 was ascribed to the Si-O vibration. Additionally, the peak at 470 cm−1 corresponded to O-Si-O vibration.

In the current work, the reverse microemulsion system was applied, in which the better controlled particles size distribution can be achieved compared to traditional Stöber method. CTZ molecules (template) combined with APTES by hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces through plentiful lone electron pairs of nitrogen and oxygen of CTZ and APTES, and then further copolymerized with CDs, which was functionalized by silica on the surface using aqueous ammonia as the catalyst. After removing the target molecules, CDs-MIPs can rebind the CTZ via the non-covalent interactions (hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces). Hence, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-MIPs can be quenched due to rebinding of CTZ.

If there exists the spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of fluorophore and the absorption spectrum of target analyte, the inner filter effect (IFE) or the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) based fluorescence quenching might occur. Once the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the molecule overlaps with the fluorescence spectrum of the fluorophore, the target molecule can absorb the fluorescence emission of the CDs-MIPs. In the present study, As observed by the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 4A), the absorption spectrum of CTZ has no obvious UV–vis absorption in the range of 350–600 nm indicating that it has no spectral overlap with the emission band (400–600 nm) of the CDs-MIPs. In addition, the fluorescence of MIPs generally can be quenched through two different mechanisms including energy transfer or photoinduced charge transfer (PET) [15,34]. And PET belongs to the dynamic quenching mechanism because such electron transfer just occurs in the excited-state and is expected to substantially change the lifetime of the fluorophore. To further investigate the quenching mechanism, fluorescence lifetime of CDs-MIPs in the absence and presence of CTZ were recorded. As demonstrated in Fig. 6 , CDs-MIPs showed a lifetime of 4.62 ns (Fig. 6A) while the presence of CTZ had obvious influences on the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs-MIPs (4.43 ns, Fig. 6B), which confirmed the dynamic quenching. Therefore, the PET mechanism is convinced as a possibility mechanism.

During the detection process, the efficiency of fluorescence quenching can be affected by various factors including the pH of the system, amount of CDs-MIPs composites and incubation time. Accordingly, the required conditions were optimized by a systematic, univariable experimental design. In addition, all the experiments were carried out in triplicate.

For examination the influence of pH on the CDs-MIPs fluorescence signal, Britton-Robinson buffer solutions were used in a range between 2 and 10. Next, solutions containing 1.0 mg CDs-MIPs with different pH in the presence 250.0 ng mL−1 CTZ were prepared and after 5 min incubation the response were measured. As observed in Fig. 7 A, the maximum quenching of the fluorescence signal was obtained at pH 7.0. At pH values of over 7.0, the silica shell as well as the core of CDs could be ionized along with that surface defects occurred, therefore the fluorescence signal was reduced. Further, when lower pH was used, the presence of hydrogen ion in the solution could affect the hydrogen bonding between CDs-MIPs and CTZ. Thus, pH 7.0 was chosen as an optimal pH solution. Additionally, the inherent stability of CDs-MIPs under different pH was also verified in supplementary materials.

Excess CDs-MIPs leads to low sensitivity to CTZ while an insufficient amount may lead to the narrow linear range. Thus, to optimize the amount of CDs-MIPs, solutions containing CDs-MIPs in a range of 200–600 µg mL−1 in the presence 250.0 ng mL−1 CTZ at pH 7.0 were prepared and the responses were recorded after 5 min incubation. As shown in Fig. 7B, the signal intensity changes could reach the highest value at the amount of 500 µg mL−1. The sensitivity of the method decreased with decreasing the amounts of CDs-MIPs since the signal to noise was also quite low while overmuch CDs-MIPs (600 µg mL−1) could lead to the self-quenching of fluorescence. Therefore, 500 µg mL−1 of CDs-MIPs was selected as an optimum amount.

Importantly, the incubation time is also an important indicator of the detection process. To evaluate the incubation time, solutions containing 500 µg mL−1 CDs-MIPs in the presence 250.0 ng mL−1 CTZ at pH 7.0 were prepared. In the following, the fluorescence signals in the range of 2–30 min were assessed. The signal intensity changes of CDs-MIPs improved up to an incubation time of 4 min and remained constant afterward (Fig. 7C). Therefore, the optimum time was determined to be 4 min.

The reusability of CDs-MIPs sensor was examined by observing the change of the fluorescence quenching at 250.0 ng mL−1 fixed CTZ solution (containing 500 µg mL−1 CDs-MIPs at pH 7.0) concentration for 6 times. After the fluorescence signal was recorded, CDs-MIPs could be recovered by centrifugation, followed by washing with excess ethanol/acetonitrile (8:2 v/v) to remove the rebinding CTZ. The CDs-MIPs was tested again with the CTZ under the same conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 8 , the sensor could be reused in CTZ detection without any appreciable loss of fluorescence quenching for 6 times. Hence, the prepared sensor can be used for at least 6 times.

Under the above optimal test conditions, the quantitative detection performance of the CDs-MIPs and CDs-NIPs towards CTZ was investigated, respectively. As reported in the previous literatures, the analyte quenched the CDs chemo-sensor relying on the concentration, which conformed to the Stern–Volmer equation: F0/F = Ksv[CCTZ] + 1, where Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and CCTZ is the concentration of CTZ. The fluorescence quenching of CDs-MIPs and towards CTZ concentration was examined by the following equation: F0/F = 1.1400 × 10−5CCTZ – 0.3352. As shown in Fig. 9 , the fluorescence intensity of CDs-MIPs obviously decreased with increasing CTZ concentration while the CDs-NIPs fluorescence intensity did not significantly change. And the specific binding affinity of CDs-MIPs towards CTZ due to the cavities in CDs-MIPs is responsible for their higher quenching efficiency or better sensitivity. Furthermore, the adsorption capacity of the CDs-MIPs and CDs-NIPs were assessed by static adsorption studies to calculate the imprinting factor (IF) in supplementary materials and the IF of the obtained CDs-MIPs was 1.9.

In addition, the relationship between F0-F and CTZ concentration was plotted and the results were displayed in Fig. 9C. A linear response to CTZ in the range of 0.5–500 ng mL−1 was achieved and the calibration equation obtained from this curve was F0–F = 1.4588C CTZ + 5001.9 (R2 = 0.9918). And the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.41 ng mL−1 regarding to the definition 3Sb/m (Sb is the standard deviation of the blank sample and m is the slope of the calibration equation). The proposed fluorescence probe was highly selective and sensitive for CTZ detection. The analytical merits of this method and other methods used for CTZ detection were shown in Table 1 . The results indicated that a lower detection limit and an appropriate wide range could be achieved by the developed method compared to other methods.

Molecular imprinting is a versatile technique for building specific recognition sites, and it is extremely important to guarantee selectivity. Selectivity of a method relates to that it could determine a particular analyte in a complex matrix without interference from other substances in the matrix. Hence, the recognition performance of the CDs-MIPs and CDs-NIPs towards other substances consisting of anion and cation (e.g. 0.3 mol L-1 K+, Mg2+, Na+, HCO3 – and the more common ions in biological samples) and some other foreign co-existing biomolecules (e.g. 1.5 µmol mL−1 carbohydrates, antioxidants, amino acids and so on) was investigated. The results suggested that CDs-MIPs were specific to CTZ but non-specific to other materials. As it can be seen from Fig. 10 , the tested molecules did not induce discernible variations in the CDs-MIPs fluorescence signal (less than 5%) which indicated that the constructed fluorescence molecularly imprinted probe could selectively detect CTZ in biological matrix.

The applicability of reported approach was studied by detecting the content of CTZ in saliva and urine samples. Different levels of CTZ concentrations were added to saliva samples (25.0 and 150.0 ng mL−1) and urine samples (50.0 and 250.0 ng mL−1) and detected in accordance with the established method (n = 3). CTZ content was measured and the recoveries were calculated and given in Table 2 . As a result, the recoveries of CTZ were between 95.8% and 99.8 % and RSDs were less than 3.0%, which implied that the proposed method is suitable for CTZ detection in real saliva and urine samples.

In summary, a florescence probe based on CDs coated with MIPs was developed for determination of CTZ in saliva and urine samples. The fluorescence of MIPs-CDs could be effectively quenched by CTZ and exhibited high sensitivity and specificity with an LOD of 0.41 ng mL−1 owing to the “key and lock” model. The green source CDs based on orange peels was firstly obtained by microwave method without using any chemicals. Afterwards, the MIPs embedded CDs was synthesized by reverse micro emulsion method. Additionally, under the optimal conditions, the good recovery of the method for determination of CTZ in real sample analysis indicated that the matrix of the saliva and urine barely has significant interference. This strategy was expected to provide an original fluorescence probe to detect trace CTZ in biological applications.

Yixuan Cui: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Software, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Aoxuan Su: Software, Validation, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Jingying Feng: Methodology, Validation, Investigation. Weichong Dong: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Junmei Li: Validation, Investigation. Huan Wang: Validation. Xiaoyu Ni: Validation. Ye Jiang: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the foundation supported by National College Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (project No. USIP2020066) for funding this research. Furthermore, we would like to thank the Department of Electron Microscopy Center, Hebei Medical University for our TEM work and we would be grateful to Chenming Zhou for his help of make TEM samples.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120293.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Water Res.

Microchem. J.
